node.title: Uber India | Onsite round | Data structure and algo

topic.title: Uber India | Onsite round | Data structure and algo

post.content:
For given list of folders, we need to find out what is the folder path for a given folder id from root.
Root folder id is always zero. no other folder can have zero as there folder ID. there would be multiple path from root folder.
A node can have multiple parents.
```
folderList = [
	{id: 0, subfolders : [7,3], name: 'abc1'},
	{id: 0, subfolders : [9], name: 'abc2'},
	{id: 9, subfolders : [], name: 'abc3'},
	{id: 3, subfolders : [2], name: 'abc4'},
	{id: 2, subfolders : [], name: 'abc5'},
	{id: 7, subfolders : [], name: 'abc6'},
	{id: 8, subfolders : [], name: 'abc7'}
];
for target folder = 2 it should return

/abc1/abc4/abc5
for target folder 8, it should return "" as it is not reachable from root.
```

What would be solution. Specially with time complexity.

I was asked this question in my phone interview for a Senior Software position in the US. I was able to solve it using a double-ended queue and BFS approach. The time complexity would be O(n) as you are touching upon each node only once. (In the question, it was guaranteed that there were no cycles when I asked a clarifying question). The second follow-up was, about how would I optimize the code to give me the path to all targets in an efficient manner. Although I didn\'t get the answer immediately, the interviewer and I came to a solution where we pre-process the input and keep traversing till the leaf nodes are reached and add all the paths along the way to a hashmap. That way, the pre-processing takes O(n) and the subsequent lookups would take just O(1).
I was asked this question in my DSA round for a Senior Software position in India

https://leetcode.com/playground/dZ9nnpKK\nAssuming we have no cycles and it is a valid subfolder path - we can model this problem as a graph problem and run a DFS / BFS. If we are to print all paths (as a follow up) - it becomes more of a backtracking problem wherein you can save each subfolder\'s path in a hashmap, so that the future lookups after a single traversal take O(1) time. For the follow up - we have to run DFS from all root folders so that we traverse and store all paths upfront. For the first question - we need not run any more DFS once we have the answer.
Can someone provide Java code? I was asked a similar question a few days back
build a `Map<Node, Set<Node>>` graph from child to Parent, and then run DFS/BFS?
Can be solved using Tries IMO

can a node have multiple parents?\n\nif there\'s a single parent, maybe we can simply traverse upwards using a child to parent mapping, till we reach the root. provided that there\'s only one path we need.

Hash Table :- search for 0 which will return 7,3 now search for 7 which will be empty so ignore search for 3 which will give you 2 and if 3 is empty then no path. This way your search will be always in constant time. Just try using unordered_map() key,value (vector<int>,string)
